map to an intersection of transformed array or tuple types.\r\n20165:         // * Otherwise we map to an object type where the type of each property has been transformed.\r\n20166:         // For instantiations with\r\n24518:     // the same origin. For example, when type parameters are in scope in an object type such as { x: T }, all\r\n24519:     // instantiations of that type have the same recursion identity. These can lie.\n\n2. **Type assertion vs narrowing interaction**: The `as` type assertion bypasses type checking entirely. A narrowed variable might get reassigned inside a callback.\n\n4. **Discriminated union narrowing edge cases**: Exhaustiveness checking can be defeated with `--exactOptionalPropertyTypes` or when types overlap.\n\n5. **typeof with non-standard values**: `typeof null === \"object\"` is a well-known JavaScript quirk.\n\n6. **`in` operator narrowing with optional/required property mismatch**: The `in` operator only checks property existence, but narrows based on the type system's understanding of the property.\n\n7. **Assignment-based narrowing unsoundness**: Closures can capture narrowed variables before TypeScript 4.4, causing the narrowing to be lost when the variable is later reassigned.\n\nNow I'm mapping out the key source files where these narrowing mechanisms are implemented—the main type checker handles the core narrowing logic, the types module defines the flow analysis structures, and utilities provide helper functions. {"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_013VvCyLi6D8EB8XPtX4sNJk","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01PmR7xYnFdu4dvhRGpishdj","name":"Write","input":{"path":"/workspace/security_review.md","content":"# Security Review: TypeScript Type Narrowing Unsoundness\n\n**Target:** TypeScript v5.7.2  \n**Date:** 2026-03-08  \n**Scope:** Control flow-based type narrowing in `src/compiler/checker.ts`\n\n---\n\n## Overview of the Narrowing Architecture\n\nTypeScript's type narrowing is implemented primarily in `src/compiler/checker.ts`, with supporting type definitions in `src/compiler/types.ts` and helper utilities in `src/compiler/utilities.ts`.\n\n### Key Files and Functions\n\n| File | Relevant Content |\n|------|-----------------|\n| `src/compiler/checker.ts` | All narrowing logic: `getFlowTypeOfReference`, `narrowType`, `narrowTypeByTypeof`, `narrowTypeByGuard`, `narrowTypeByEquality`, `narrowTypeByInstanceof`, `narrowTypeByInKeyword` |\n| `src/compiler/types.ts` | `TypeFacts`, `FlowType`, `IncompleteType` (line ~4256), `FlowCondition`, `FlowSwitchClause`, `FlowCall` |\n| `src/compiler/utilities.ts` | `isMatchingReference`, `containsMatchingReference`, type guard helpers |\n| `src/compiler/binder.ts` | Flow graph construction (control flow state, line ~534) |\n\n### Narrowing Entry Points\n\nThe primary entry point for narrowing is **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), called from property access type resolution (line ~18556) and from `getFlowTypeOfDestructuring` (line ~11068). Control flow type analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects internally during loop analysis — these have a `flags` of zero and never escape the function (see `src/compiler/types.ts`, line 4252–4259).\n\nThe narrowing pipeline:\n\n1. **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** — walks the flow graph backward from the current reference point\n2. **`narrowType`** — dispatches to specific narrowing functions based on condition kind\n3. **`narrowTypeByTypeof`** / **`narrowTypeByGuard`** / **`narrowTypeByEquality`** / **`narrowTypeByInstanceof`** / **`narrowTypeByInKeyword`** — handle individual narrowing patterns\n\nTypeFacts (bit flags in `src/compiler/types.ts`) such as `TypeFacts.EQUndefined` (used at line ~11042 in checker.ts) encode runtime type facts that the type system reasons about.\n\n---\n\n## Findings: Unsound Narrowing Patterns\n\n### Finding 1: User-Defined Type Guard Unsoundness (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature`\n\n**Description:**  \nTypeScript fully trusts user-defined type guard functions (predicates of the form `x is T`). The compiler never verifies that the function body actually validates the asserted type. This allows any function to arbitrarily lie about the type of a value, completely bypassing structural type checking.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\n// Unsound type guard: claims to check for string but always returns true\nfunction isString(x: unknown): x is string {\n    return true; // No actual check!\n}\n\nfunction process(val: string | number): void {\n    if (isString(val)) {\n        // TypeScript narrows val to `string` here\n        // But at runtime val may be a number\n        console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Runtime error if val is number\n    }\n}\n\nprocess(42); // Passes type checking, crashes at runtime\n```\n\n**More dangerous variant — cross-type guard:**\n\n```typescript\ninterface Admin { role: \"admin\"; permissions: string[] }\ninterface Guest { role: \"guest\" }\n\nfunction isAdmin(user: Admin | Guest): user is Admin {\n    // Bug: reversed check\n    return (user as any).role !== \"admin\";\n}\n\nfunction deleteUser(user: Admin | Guest) {\n    if (isAdmin(user)) {\n        // TypeScript thinks this is Admin, but it's actually Guest\n        user.permissions.forEach(p => revoke(p)); // Runtime error: permissions undefined\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**  \nIn `narrowTypeByGuard` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), when a call expression matches a function with a `TypePredicate`, the compiler applies the predicate type directly without validating that the function implementation is sound. The predicate is retrieved via `getTypePredicateOfSignature` and trusted unconditionally.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**  \nThis is a fundamental unsoundness. Any developer can write a lying type guard, and the compiler provides no defense. Malicious or buggy type guards in third-party libraries can silently corrupt type safety at call sites.\n\n**Recommendation:**  \n- Add an optional `--strictTypeGuards` mode that performs basic structural validation of type guard return paths.\n- Document that type guards are user-asserted and not compiler-verified.\n- Consider warning when a type guard body contains an unconditional `return true` / `return false`.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 2: Control Flow Narrowing Lost Across Function Boundaries (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `getFlowTypeOfReference`, `narrowType` (FlowCall handling)\n\n**Description:**  \nTypeScript deliberately does not propagate narrowed types into or out of function calls. A variable narrowed before a callback invocation may be re-widened after the call returns, even if the callback does not modify the variable. More critically, a narrowed variable captured in a closure may be mutated inside the closure, causing the narrowed type to be invalid when accessed afterward.\n\n**Code Example — closure mutation:**\n\n```typescript\nfunction example(x: string | number): void {\n    if (typeof x === \"string\") {\n        // x is narrowed to string here\n        setTimeout(() => {\n            // TypeScript sees x as string | number inside callback\n            // (narrowing is NOT preserved through closures)\n        }, 0);\n        \n        // x is still narrowed to string here — correct\n        console.log(x.toUpperCase()); // Safe\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**More dangerous — variable mutation through aliased callback:**\n\n```typescript\nlet val: string | number = \"hello\";\n\nfunction runCallback(cb: () => void): void {\n    cb();\n}\n\nif (typeof val === \"string\") {\n    // val is narrowed to string\n    runCallback(() => {\n        val = 42; // Reassigns val to number\n    });\n    // TypeScript still considers val to be string here!\n    // This IS caught in simple cases, but the analysis has limits\n    console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Potential runtime error\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**  \n`getFlowTypeOfReference` processes `FlowCall` nodes, which represent function calls that could mutate captured variables. The checker widens types conservatively after calls to functions that could cause mutations — but the analysis is interprocedural only at the call node level. Complex aliasing patterns involving closures returned from functions or stored in objects are not tracked.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**  \nIn most simple cases TypeScript correctly widens after calls. The unsoundness arises in complex alias patterns or with non-local mutation through shared mutable state.\n\n**Recommendation:**  \n- Improve the `FlowCall` analysis to track whether a passed callback has write access to the narrowed variable.\n- Consider treating all closures that capture a variable as potentially invalidating the narrowing for that variable after the call.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 3: `typeof` Narrowing with `null` (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByTypeof`\n\n**Description:**  \nJavaScript's `typeof null === \"object\"` is a well-known quirk. TypeScript's `narrowTypeByTypeof` function does **not** narrow `null` out of the type when the condition `typeof x === \"object\"` is true. This means that after `typeof x === \"object\"`, the type still includes `null`, which is correct — but developers often assume it eliminates `null`.\n\nHowever, there is a subtler related issue: TypeScript allows narrowing with custom `typeof`-like checks through user-defined type guards (see Finding 1), so any `typeof`-like narrowing that relies on user-provided logic is equally unsound.\n\n**More impactful unsoundness — `typeof` on generic types:**\n\n```typescript\nfunction process<T>(value: T): void {\n    if (typeof value === \"string\") {\n        // TypeScript narrows T to string & T here\n        // But if T is constrained to `object`, this branch is unreachable\n        // yet TypeScript still enters and narrows\n        const s: string = value; // TypeScript accepts this\n        console.log(s.length);   // Safe if branch is actually reachable\n    }\n}\n\n// Called with a type that makes the typeof check always false\nclass Foo {}\nprocess<Foo>(new Foo()); // typeof (new Foo()) === \"object\", not \"string\"\n                          // TypeScript accepts this - branch simply not entered\n```\n\n**Genuine unsoundness — `typeof` with branded types:**\n\n```typescript\ntype BrandedString = string & { __brand: \"safe\" };\n\nfunction isBranded(x: unknown): x is BrandedString {\n    return typeof x === \"string\"; // Lies about the brand\n}\n\nconst dangerous: unknown = \"malicious\";\nif (isBranded(dangerous)) {\n    // x is now typed as BrandedString, but brand was never verified\n    useOnlyBranded(dangerous); // Type-safe according to TypeScript, unsound in reality\n}\n\ndeclare function useOnlyBranded(s: BrandedString): void;\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**  \nIn `narrowTypeByTypeof` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), the narrowing maps `typeof` string literals to type predicates (e.g., `\"string\"` → `TypeFacts.TypeofEQString`). The function correctly handles `\"object\"` to not remove `null`, but the interaction with branded/nominal types is not validated — the brand is part of the structural type, not tracked at runtime.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**  \nBranded types used for security-sensitive validation (safe strings, sanitized HTML, validated inputs) can be counterfeited by any code that lies about passing a `typeof` check.\n\n**Recommendation:**  \n- Provide a mechanism for \"opaque\" or \"nominal\" types that cannot be produced by type assertions or lying type guards.\n- Document that branded types do not provide runtime safety guarantees.\n- Consider a `--strictBrands` compiler option that validates that branded type constructors are sealed.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 4: Discriminated Union Narrowing with Excess Properties (Low–Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByDiscriminant`, `findMatchingDiscriminantType`\n\n**Description:**  \nTypeScript's discriminant-based union narrowing relies on checking the type of a discriminant property (e.g., `kind: \"foo\" | \"bar\"`). However, this narrowing can interact unsoundly with excess property checking when types are constructed via type assertions (`as`).\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Circle = { kind: \"circle\"; radius: number };\ntype Square = { kind: \"square\"; side: number };\ntype Shape = Circle | Square;\n\nfunction area(shape: Shape): number {\n    if (shape.kind === \"circle\") {\n        // shape is narrowed to Circle\n        return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2;\n    }\n    // shape is narrowed to Square\n    return shape.side ** 2;\n}\n\n// Unsound: type assertion bypasses structural checking\nconst corrupted = { kind: \"circle\" } as Shape; // Missing radius!\narea(corrupted); // Narrowed to Circle, but radius is undefined → NaN\n```\n\n**More subtle — discriminant narrowing with literal widening:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Tagged<T extends string, D> = { tag: T; data: D };\ntype NumberTag = Tagged<\"number\", number>;\ntype StringTag = Tagged<\"string\", string>;\ntype AnyTag = NumberTag | StringTag;\n\nfunction processTag(t: AnyTag): string {\n    switch (t.tag) {\n        case \"number\": return t.data.toFixed(2); // Narrowed to NumberTag\n        case \"string\": return t.data.toUpperCase(); // Narrowed to StringTag\n        default:\n            const _exhaustive: never = t;\n            return \"\";\n    }\n}\n\n// A tag with a widened discriminant bypasses exhaustiveness\nconst bad = { tag: \"number\" as string, data: \"not a number\" } as AnyTag;\nprocessTag(bad); // Falls through to default despite tag === \"number\"\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**  \nThe `narrowTypeByDiscriminant` function trusts the type system's representation of the discriminant property. Type assertions (`as`) can create values whose runtime structure doesn't match their declared type. The narrowing then applies the discriminant-narrowed type (e.g., `Circle`) even though the runtime object doesn't satisfy all of `Circle`'s properties.\n\n**Severity:** **LOW–MEDIUM**  \nThis requires the developer to first bypass structural checking with `as`. However, it's common in practice for developers to use `as` to construct test fixtures, mocked values, or to work around overly strict types — and the resulting narrowing errors can be hard to debug.\n\n**Recommendation:**  \n- Emit a warning when a type assertion (`as`) is used on a discriminated union type and the asserted type doesn't fully satisfy the structural requirements of the discriminant.\n- Document that discriminated union narrowing is only as sound as the runtime values it's applied to.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 5: Type Assertion Interaction with Narrowing (`as` Cascading) (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowType`, `checkAsExpression`\n\n**Description:**  \nTypeScript's `as` operator is a pure compile-time assertion that performs no runtime check. When a narrowed type is subsequently widened via `as`, and then narrowed again, the chain of narrowings can produce a type that is completely unrelated to the actual runtime value.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\nfunction processValue(x: string | number | boolean): void {\n    // Step 1: Narrow to string\n    if (typeof x === \"string\") {\n        // Step 2: Widen via assertion to get \"unknown-as-string\" type\n        const widened = x as unknown as { secret: () => void };\n        // Step 3: Use unsound narrowed type\n        widened.secret(); // TypeScript accepts; crashes at runtime\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Double-assertion bypass pattern (extremely dangerous):**\n\n```typescript\ninterface SecureToken {\n    readonly __tokenBrand: unique symbol;\n    value: string;\n}\n\ndeclare function useToken(token: SecureToken): void;\n\n// Bypasses the unique symbol brand entirely\nconst fakeToken = \"malicious\" as unknown as SecureToken;\nuseToken(fakeToken); // Type-safe, but runtime value is a plain string\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**  \n`checkAsExpression` in `src/compiler/checker.ts` permits the \"double cast\" pattern (`x as unknown as T`) as a deliberate escape hatch, documented as such. The narrowing system then treats the asserted type as ground truth. There is no record of the assertion chain, so subsequent narrowing operates on the asserted (potentially false) type.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**  \nThe double-assertion pattern (`x as unknown as T`) is widely used to bypass type safety. It is well-documented as an escape hatch but is commonly misused. Library authors who use `unique symbol` brands for security tokens or capabilities can have those brands counterfeited with a single assertion.\n\n**Recommendation:**  \n- Emit a warning (behind a flag like `--warnOnDoubleAssertion`) for the `as unknown as T` pattern.\n- Consider a `const` brand type feature that makes brand symbols unforgeably — not expressible via assertions.\n- Add a lint rule or compiler option that prevents type assertions from crossing certain security boundaries.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Pattern | Functions | Severity |\n|---|---------|-----------|----------|\n| 1 | User-defined type guard lying | `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature` | **HIGH** |\n| 2 | Control flow lost across function boundaries | `getFlowTypeOfReference`, FlowCall handling | **MEDIUM** |\n| 3 | `typeof` narrowing with branded types | `narrowTypeByTypeof` | **MEDIUM** |\n| 4 | Discriminated union + type assertion | `narrowTypeByDiscriminant` | **LOW–MEDIUM** |\n| 5 | Double type assertion cascading into narrowing | `checkAsExpression`, `narrowType` | **HIGH** |\n\n---\n\n## Architectural Observations\n\n1. **TypeFacts are sound, but trust is misplaced upstream.** The `TypeFacts` bitmask system (used throughout narrowing in `src/compiler/checker.ts`, e.g., at line ~11042 with `TypeFacts.EQUndefined`) encodes correct runtime fact semantics. The unsoundness arises not from TypeFacts themselves, but from the trust placed in user-provided evidence (type guards, assertions) that supplies the input to TypeFacts-based reasoning.\n\n2. **`IncompleteType` during loop analysis.** Loop analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects (`src/compiler/types.ts`, line 4252–4259) internally within `getFlowTypeOfReference` to handle forward references. These are correctly contained and never escape the function, so they don't represent a security concern.\n\n3. **Interprocedural analysis is deliberately limited.** TypeScript does not analyze function bodies when called. `FlowCall` nodes trigger conservative widening at call sites (for functions that might mutate captured variables), but the analysis doesn't track aliased writes through closures returned from or stored by functions.\n\n4. **`strictNullChecks` is the main defense.** Most of the null/undefined-related narrowing unsoundness is gated behind `strictNullChecks`. Programs not using this flag have significantly weaker type safety guarantees.\n\n---\n\n## Recommendations Summary\n\n1. **Add `--strictTypeGuards` option** to validate that type guard function bodies actually narrow the input to the asserted type (e.g., by requiring at least one `typeof`, `instanceof`, or discriminant check in the return path).\n\n2. **Add `--warnOnDoubleAssertion` option** to flag `x as unknown as T` patterns that are commonly used to bypass type safety.\n\n3. **Provide opaque/nominal types** natively so that security-sensitive brand types cannot be produced by type assertions.\n\n4. **Improve closure mutation analysis** in `getFlowTypeOfReference` to better track when a closure captures and modifies a narrowed variable.\n\n5. **Document narrowing limitations** prominently in the TypeScript Handbook, particularly for security-sensitive patterns involving type guards, branded types, and discriminated unions.\n"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":1,"cache_creation_input_tokens":6544,"cache_read_input_tokens":143245,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":6544,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":8,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"756d1c78-0a27-4ea9-92b3-a4b6e6aaab0e","uuid":"f53690c5-5ee1-430c-a351-5d7c5febd7b5"}

{"type":"assistant","message":{"model":"claude-sonnet-4-6","id":"msg_01CtmAob8Mqo2dVtUWMeSoYj","type":"message","role":"assistant","content":[{"type":"tool_use","id":"toolu_01FQE8AQpbm1aQUr3CR7vJJ4","name":"Write","input":{"file_path":"/workspace/security_review.md","content":"# Security Review: TypeScript Type Narrowing Unsoundness\n\n**Target:** TypeScript v5.7.2\n**Date:** 2026-03-08\n**Scope:** Control flow-based type narrowing in `src/compiler/checker.ts`\n\n---\n\n## Overview of the Narrowing Architecture\n\nTypeScript's type narrowing is implemented primarily in `src/compiler/checker.ts`, with supporting type definitions in `src/compiler/types.ts` and helper utilities in `src/compiler/utilities.ts`.\n\n### Key Files and Functions\n\n| File | Relevant Content |\n|------|-----------------|\n| `src/compiler/checker.ts` | All narrowing logic: `getFlowTypeOfReference`, `narrowType`, `narrowTypeByTypeof`, `narrowTypeByGuard`, `narrowTypeByEquality`, `narrowTypeByInstanceof`, `narrowTypeByInKeyword` |\n| `src/compiler/types.ts` | `TypeFacts`, `FlowType`, `IncompleteType` (line ~4256), `FlowCondition`, `FlowSwitchClause`, `FlowCall` |\n| `src/compiler/utilities.ts` | `isMatchingReference`, `containsMatchingReference`, type guard helpers |\n| `src/compiler/binder.ts` | Flow graph construction (control flow state, line ~534) |\n\n### Narrowing Entry Points\n\nThe primary entry point for narrowing is **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), called from property access type resolution (line ~18556) and from `getFlowTypeOfDestructuring` (line ~11068). Control flow type analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects internally during loop analysis — these have a `flags` of zero and never escape the function (see `src/compiler/types.ts`, lines 4252–4259).\n\nThe narrowing pipeline:\n\n1. **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** — walks the flow graph backward from the current reference point\n2. **`narrowType`** — dispatches to specific narrowing functions based on condition kind\n3. **`narrowTypeByTypeof`** / **`narrowTypeByGuard`** / **`narrowTypeByEquality`** / **`narrowTypeByInstanceof`** / **`narrowTypeByInKeyword`** — handle individual narrowing patterns\n\n`TypeFacts` (bit flags, e.g., `TypeFacts.EQUndefined` used at line ~11042 in checker.ts) encode runtime type facts that the type system reasons about.\n\n---\n\n## Findings: Unsound Narrowing Patterns\n\n### Finding 1: User-Defined Type Guard Unsoundness (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript fully trusts user-defined type guard functions (predicates of the form `x is T`). The compiler never verifies that the function body actually validates the asserted type. Any function can arbitrarily lie about the type of a value, completely bypassing structural type checking.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\n// Unsound type guard: claims to check for string but always returns true\nfunction isString(x: unknown): x is string {\n    return true; // No actual check — always passes\n}\n\nfunction process(val: string | number): void {\n    if (isString(val)) {\n        // TypeScript narrows val to `string` here\n        // But at runtime val may still be a number\n        console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Runtime TypeError if val is 42\n    }\n}\n\nprocess(42); // Passes type checking, crashes at runtime\n```\n\n**More dangerous variant — cross-type guard with wrong logic:**\n\n```typescript\ninterface Admin { role: \"admin\"; permissions: string[] }\ninterface Guest { role: \"guest\" }\n\nfunction isAdmin(user: Admin | Guest): user is Admin {\n    return (user as any).role !== \"admin\"; // Inverted check: always returns Guest as Admin\n}\n\nfunction deleteUser(user: Admin | Guest) {\n    if (isAdmin(user)) {\n        // TypeScript believes this is Admin\n        user.permissions.forEach(p => revoke(p)); // Runtime error: permissions undefined on Guest\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\nIn `narrowTypeByGuard` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), when a call expression matches a function with a `TypePredicate`, the predicate type is applied directly without validating that the function implementation is sound. The predicate is retrieved via `getTypePredicateOfSignature` and trusted unconditionally.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**\nThis is a fundamental design unsoundness. Buggy or malicious type guards in third-party libraries can silently corrupt type safety at all call sites without any compiler warning.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Add an optional `--strictTypeGuards` mode that validates type guard return paths contain at least one structural check.\n- Warn when a type guard body contains an unconditional `return true` or `return false`.\n- Document prominently that type guards are user-asserted, not compiler-verified.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 2: Control Flow Narrowing Lost Across Function Boundaries (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `getFlowTypeOfReference`, FlowCall node handling\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript does not propagate narrowed types across arbitrary function call boundaries. A variable narrowed before a function call is widened back to its declared type after that call, because the called function might mutate the variable. In practice, the analysis can be defeated by passing a closure that mutates a captured narrowed variable — after the call returns, the stale narrowed type is still used.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\nlet val: string | number = \"hello\";\n\nfunction runCallback(cb: () => void): void {\n    cb();\n}\n\nif (typeof val === \"string\") {\n    // val is narrowed to `string` here\n    runCallback(() => {\n        val = 42; // Reassigns val to number inside the callback\n    });\n    // TypeScript still considers val to be `string` after runCallback returns\n    console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Runtime error: 42 has no toUpperCase\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`getFlowTypeOfReference` processes `FlowCall` nodes to conservatively widen types for variables that might be mutated by a called function. However, the analysis tracks only direct references to the variable at the call site and does not account for closures that capture the variable by reference and mutate it asynchronously or via aliased paths. After the call, the narrowed type persists in some cases where it should not.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**\nMost simple cases are handled correctly. The unsoundness manifests with complex alias patterns where TypeScript cannot statically determine that the callback has write access to the narrowed variable.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Improve `FlowCall` analysis to conservatively widen any variable captured (by-reference) in a passed closure argument.\n- Consider treating all closures passed to functions as potentially invalidating narrowing on any variable they close over.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 3: `typeof` Narrowing with Branded/Nominal Types (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByTypeof`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript uses branded (nominal) types — intersections of primitives with an object literal containing a `unique symbol` property — as a pattern for encoding type-level invariants (e.g., sanitized strings, validated inputs, security tokens). The `narrowTypeByTypeof` function does not validate brands; it narrows only the primitive layer. A lying type guard that merely checks `typeof x === \"string\"` can promote an unbranded string to a fully branded type.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\ntype SafeHtml = string & { readonly __brand: unique symbol };\n\n// Lying type guard that ignores the brand\nfunction isSafeHtml(x: unknown): x is SafeHtml {\n    return typeof x === \"string\"; // Only checks primitive type, not brand\n}\n\ndeclare function renderHtml(html: SafeHtml): void;\n\nconst userInput: unknown = \"<script>alert(1)</script>\";\nif (isSafeHtml(userInput)) {\n    renderHtml(userInput); // Type-safe per TypeScript, but brand was never validated\n    // Potential XSS vulnerability\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`narrowTypeByTypeof` maps `typeof` string literals to `TypeFacts` predicates (e.g., `\"string\"` → `TypeFacts.TypeofEQString`). This correctly narrows the primitive base type, but brands are structural intersections — invisible at runtime — so they are never validated by the `typeof` check. The combination with a type guard that asserts the branded type allows the runtime primitive to masquerade as a branded type.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**\nWhen branded types are used for security-sensitive invariants (safe SQL, safe HTML, validated tokens), a lying type guard can create counterfeits. The impact is application-level, not compiler-level.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Provide a native \"opaque\" type feature that cannot be produced by type assertions or lying type guards.\n- Document that branded types do not provide runtime safety guarantees and are not enforced by `narrowTypeByTypeof`.\n- Consider a `--strictBrands` option that requires branded type production to go through designated constructor functions only.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 4: Discriminated Union Narrowing via Type Assertion (Low–Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByDiscriminant`, `findMatchingDiscriminantType`\n\n**Description:**\nDiscriminated union narrowing relies on the discriminant property having a literal type that matches one of the union members. When a value is constructed via a type assertion (`as`), excess property checking is suppressed and missing required properties are not flagged. After narrowing, the compiler treats the object as fully satisfying all properties of the narrowed union member, even if those properties are missing at runtime.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Circle = { kind: \"circle\"; radius: number };\ntype Square = { kind: \"square\"; side: number };\ntype Shape = Circle | Square;\n\nfunction area(shape: Shape): number {\n    if (shape.kind === \"circle\") {\n        return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2; // radius may be undefined at runtime\n    }\n    return shape.side ** 2;\n}\n\n// Type assertion bypasses structural checking for `radius`\nconst broken = { kind: \"circle\" } as Shape;\narea(broken); // Narrowed to Circle, but radius is undefined → NaN at runtime\n```\n\n**Exhaustiveness checking defeated:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Result = { status: \"ok\"; data: string } | { status: \"error\"; message: string };\n\nfunction handle(r: Result): string {\n    switch (r.status) {\n        case \"ok\": return r.data.toUpperCase();\n        case \"error\": return r.message;\n        default:\n            const _never: never = r;\n            return \"\";\n    }\n}\n\n// Assertion with widened discriminant bypasses exhaustiveness\nconst bad = { status: \"ok\" as string, data: 42 } as Result;\nhandle(bad); // Enters \"ok\" branch, calls toUpperCase() on number 42 → RuntimeError\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`narrowTypeByDiscriminant` trusts the static type of the discriminant property. Type assertions (`as`) in `src/compiler/checker.ts` (`checkAsExpression`) perform only a subset of structural checks and suppress excess property errors. The narrowed type then claims full structural satisfaction of the union member.\n\n**Severity:** **LOW–MEDIUM**\nRequires prior use of `as`. Common in practice for test fixtures, mock objects, and working around overly strict types.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Warn when a type assertion is applied to a discriminated union type and the asserted concrete member has properties that are structurally absent from the source type.\n- Emit a note at discriminant narrowing sites when the value's declared type was produced via an assertion.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 5: Double Type Assertion Cascading into Narrowing (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `checkAsExpression`, `narrowType`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript intentionally permits the \"double cast\" pattern (`x as unknown as T`) as an escape hatch for cases where the programmer knows the type is correct but the compiler cannot verify it. Once this assertion is applied, all subsequent narrowing operates on the asserted (potentially false) type as ground truth. There is no record of the assertion chain, so the unsoundness is invisible to downstream code.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\n// Dangerous: unique symbol brand is supposed to be unforgeable\ninterface SecureToken {\n    readonly __tokenBrand: unique symbol;\n    readonly value: string;\n}\n\ndeclare function useToken(token: SecureToken): void;\n\n// Bypass the unique symbol brand with a double assertion\nconst fakeToken = \"malicious-string\" as unknown as SecureToken;\nuseToken(fakeToken); // Fully type-safe per TypeScript, brand was never created\n```\n\n**Cascading narrowing after double assertion:**\n\n```typescript\nfunction processShape(x: string | number): void {\n    if (typeof x === \"string\") {\n        // x is narrowed to string\n        const corrupted = x as unknown as { exec: () => string };\n        // TypeScript now reasons about corrupted as { exec: () => string }\n        // All subsequent narrowing applied to `corrupted` is invalid\n        corrupted.exec(); // Runtime TypeError\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`checkAsExpression` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`) permits any expression to be asserted as any type as long as one side is assignable to or from the other, OR as long as both sides go through `unknown` (the double-cast trick). Once asserted, the narrowing functions (`narrowType`, etc.) have no way to detect that the type's provenance was an assertion rather than a sound derivation.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**\nThe double-assertion pattern is common in TypeScript codebases and is treated as an escape hatch. However, it fundamentally breaks the type system's soundness guarantees. Security-sensitive patterns that rely on `unique symbol` brands (capability-based security, CSRF tokens, sanitized strings) are particularly vulnerable.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Add a `--warnOnDoubleAssertion` flag to emit a warning for `x as unknown as T` patterns.\n- Explore \"sealing\" unique-symbol branded types so they cannot be produced via assertion.\n- Consider tracking assertion provenance in the type system so that narrowing can optionally refuse to further refine assertion-derived types.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Pattern | Primary Functions | Severity |\n|---|---------|-------------------|----------|\n| 1 | User-defined type guard lying | `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature` | **HIGH** |\n| 2 | Control flow narrowing lost across function boundaries | `getFlowTypeOfReference`, FlowCall handling | **MEDIUM** |\n| 3 | `typeof` narrowing with branded types | `narrowTypeByTypeof` | **MEDIUM** |\n| 4 | Discriminated union + type assertion | `narrowTypeByDiscriminant`, `checkAsExpression` | **LOW–MEDIUM** |\n| 5 | Double type assertion cascading into narrowing | `checkAsExpression`, `narrowType` | **HIGH** |\n\n---\n\n## Architectural Observations\n\n1. **TypeFacts are internally sound.** The `TypeFacts` bitmask system (used throughout narrowing, e.g., `TypeFacts.EQUndefined` at line ~11042 in `checker.ts`) correctly encodes runtime type semantics. Unsoundness arises not from TypeFacts themselves but from the trust placed in user-provided evidence (type guards, assertions).\n\n2. **`IncompleteType` is safely contained.** Loop analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects (`src/compiler/types.ts`, lines 4252–4259) internally within `getFlowTypeOfReference`. These have `flags = 0` and are explicitly documented as never escaping the function — no security concern.\n\n3. **Interprocedural analysis is deliberately limited.** TypeScript does not analyze function bodies at call sites. `FlowCall` nodes (`src/compiler/binder.ts`, line ~534) trigger conservative widening, but the analysis does not track aliased writes through closures or objects. This is a deliberate scalability trade-off that creates narrowing soundness gaps.\n\n4. **`strictNullChecks` is a major defense boundary.** Programs not using `--strictNullChecks` have significantly weaker guarantees. All null/undefined-related narrowing unsoundness is amplified without this flag.\n\n5. **Type assertions are the root of most unsoundness.** The `as` operator in `checkAsExpression` is the primary source of narrowing unsoundness because it allows injecting arbitrary types into the system. All five findings above either directly involve assertions or are exacerbated by them.\n"},"caller":{"type":"direct"}}],"stop_reason":null,"stop_sequence":null,"usage":{"input_tokens":3,"cache_creation_input_tokens":374,"cache_read_input_tokens":155212,"cache_creation":{"ephemeral_5m_input_tokens":374,"ephemeral_1h_input_tokens":0},"output_tokens":1,"service_tier":"standard","inference_geo":"not_available"},"context_management":null},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"756d1c78-0a27-4ea9-92b3-a4b6e6aaab0e","uuid":"92ae5acf-3c23-4e55-a08b-ddca48ff0a75"}
{"type":"user","message":{"role":"user","content":[{"tool_use_id":"toolu_01FQE8AQpbm1aQUr3CR7vJJ4","type":"tool_result","content":"File created successfully at: /workspace/security_review.md"}]},"parent_tool_use_id":null,"session_id":"756d1c78-0a27-4ea9-92b3-a4b6e6aaab0e","uuid":"3fd0e5cd-2ff4-45bc-85d7-b5c5f4ac22e9","tool_use_result":{"type":"create","filePath":"/workspace/security_review.md","content":"# Security Review: TypeScript Type Narrowing Unsoundness\n\n**Target:** TypeScript v5.7.2\n**Date:** 2026-03-08\n**Scope:** Control flow-based type narrowing in `src/compiler/checker.ts`\n\n---\n\n## Overview of the Narrowing Architecture\n\nTypeScript's type narrowing is implemented primarily in `src/compiler/checker.ts`, with supporting type definitions in `src/compiler/types.ts` and helper utilities in `src/compiler/utilities.ts`.\n\n### Key Files and Functions\n\n| File | Relevant Content |\n|------|-----------------|\n| `src/compiler/checker.ts` | All narrowing logic: `getFlowTypeOfReference`, `narrowType`, `narrowTypeByTypeof`, `narrowTypeByGuard`, `narrowTypeByEquality`, `narrowTypeByInstanceof`, `narrowTypeByInKeyword` |\n| `src/compiler/types.ts` | `TypeFacts`, `FlowType`, `IncompleteType` (line ~4256), `FlowCondition`, `FlowSwitchClause`, `FlowCall` |\n| `src/compiler/utilities.ts` | `isMatchingReference`, `containsMatchingReference`, type guard helpers |\n| `src/compiler/binder.ts` | Flow graph construction (control flow state, line ~534) |\n\n### Narrowing Entry Points\n\nThe primary entry point for narrowing is **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), called from property access type resolution (line ~18556) and from `getFlowTypeOfDestructuring` (line ~11068). Control flow type analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects internally during loop analysis — these have a `flags` of zero and never escape the function (see `src/compiler/types.ts`, lines 4252–4259).\n\nThe narrowing pipeline:\n\n1. **`getFlowTypeOfReference`** — walks the flow graph backward from the current reference point\n2. **`narrowType`** — dispatches to specific narrowing functions based on condition kind\n3. **`narrowTypeByTypeof`** / **`narrowTypeByGuard`** / **`narrowTypeByEquality`** / **`narrowTypeByInstanceof`** / **`narrowTypeByInKeyword`** — handle individual narrowing patterns\n\n`TypeFacts` (bit flags, e.g., `TypeFacts.EQUndefined` used at line ~11042 in checker.ts) encode runtime type facts that the type system reasons about.\n\n---\n\n## Findings: Unsound Narrowing Patterns\n\n### Finding 1: User-Defined Type Guard Unsoundness (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript fully trusts user-defined type guard functions (predicates of the form `x is T`). The compiler never verifies that the function body actually validates the asserted type. Any function can arbitrarily lie about the type of a value, completely bypassing structural type checking.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\n// Unsound type guard: claims to check for string but always returns true\nfunction isString(x: unknown): x is string {\n    return true; // No actual check — always passes\n}\n\nfunction process(val: string | number): void {\n    if (isString(val)) {\n        // TypeScript narrows val to `string` here\n        // But at runtime val may still be a number\n        console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Runtime TypeError if val is 42\n    }\n}\n\nprocess(42); // Passes type checking, crashes at runtime\n```\n\n**More dangerous variant — cross-type guard with wrong logic:**\n\n```typescript\ninterface Admin { role: \"admin\"; permissions: string[] }\ninterface Guest { role: \"guest\" }\n\nfunction isAdmin(user: Admin | Guest): user is Admin {\n    return (user as any).role !== \"admin\"; // Inverted check: always returns Guest as Admin\n}\n\nfunction deleteUser(user: Admin | Guest) {\n    if (isAdmin(user)) {\n        // TypeScript believes this is Admin\n        user.permissions.forEach(p => revoke(p)); // Runtime error: permissions undefined on Guest\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\nIn `narrowTypeByGuard` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`), when a call expression matches a function with a `TypePredicate`, the predicate type is applied directly without validating that the function implementation is sound. The predicate is retrieved via `getTypePredicateOfSignature` and trusted unconditionally.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**\nThis is a fundamental design unsoundness. Buggy or malicious type guards in third-party libraries can silently corrupt type safety at all call sites without any compiler warning.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Add an optional `--strictTypeGuards` mode that validates type guard return paths contain at least one structural check.\n- Warn when a type guard body contains an unconditional `return true` or `return false`.\n- Document prominently that type guards are user-asserted, not compiler-verified.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 2: Control Flow Narrowing Lost Across Function Boundaries (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `getFlowTypeOfReference`, FlowCall node handling\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript does not propagate narrowed types across arbitrary function call boundaries. A variable narrowed before a function call is widened back to its declared type after that call, because the called function might mutate the variable. In practice, the analysis can be defeated by passing a closure that mutates a captured narrowed variable — after the call returns, the stale narrowed type is still used.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\nlet val: string | number = \"hello\";\n\nfunction runCallback(cb: () => void): void {\n    cb();\n}\n\nif (typeof val === \"string\") {\n    // val is narrowed to `string` here\n    runCallback(() => {\n        val = 42; // Reassigns val to number inside the callback\n    });\n    // TypeScript still considers val to be `string` after runCallback returns\n    console.log(val.toUpperCase()); // Runtime error: 42 has no toUpperCase\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`getFlowTypeOfReference` processes `FlowCall` nodes to conservatively widen types for variables that might be mutated by a called function. However, the analysis tracks only direct references to the variable at the call site and does not account for closures that capture the variable by reference and mutate it asynchronously or via aliased paths. After the call, the narrowed type persists in some cases where it should not.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**\nMost simple cases are handled correctly. The unsoundness manifests with complex alias patterns where TypeScript cannot statically determine that the callback has write access to the narrowed variable.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Improve `FlowCall` analysis to conservatively widen any variable captured (by-reference) in a passed closure argument.\n- Consider treating all closures passed to functions as potentially invalidating narrowing on any variable they close over.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 3: `typeof` Narrowing with Branded/Nominal Types (Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByTypeof`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript uses branded (nominal) types — intersections of primitives with an object literal containing a `unique symbol` property — as a pattern for encoding type-level invariants (e.g., sanitized strings, validated inputs, security tokens). The `narrowTypeByTypeof` function does not validate brands; it narrows only the primitive layer. A lying type guard that merely checks `typeof x === \"string\"` can promote an unbranded string to a fully branded type.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\ntype SafeHtml = string & { readonly __brand: unique symbol };\n\n// Lying type guard that ignores the brand\nfunction isSafeHtml(x: unknown): x is SafeHtml {\n    return typeof x === \"string\"; // Only checks primitive type, not brand\n}\n\ndeclare function renderHtml(html: SafeHtml): void;\n\nconst userInput: unknown = \"<script>alert(1)</script>\";\nif (isSafeHtml(userInput)) {\n    renderHtml(userInput); // Type-safe per TypeScript, but brand was never validated\n    // Potential XSS vulnerability\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`narrowTypeByTypeof` maps `typeof` string literals to `TypeFacts` predicates (e.g., `\"string\"` → `TypeFacts.TypeofEQString`). This correctly narrows the primitive base type, but brands are structural intersections — invisible at runtime — so they are never validated by the `typeof` check. The combination with a type guard that asserts the branded type allows the runtime primitive to masquerade as a branded type.\n\n**Severity:** **MEDIUM**\nWhen branded types are used for security-sensitive invariants (safe SQL, safe HTML, validated tokens), a lying type guard can create counterfeits. The impact is application-level, not compiler-level.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Provide a native \"opaque\" type feature that cannot be produced by type assertions or lying type guards.\n- Document that branded types do not provide runtime safety guarantees and are not enforced by `narrowTypeByTypeof`.\n- Consider a `--strictBrands` option that requires branded type production to go through designated constructor functions only.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 4: Discriminated Union Narrowing via Type Assertion (Low–Medium Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `narrowTypeByDiscriminant`, `findMatchingDiscriminantType`\n\n**Description:**\nDiscriminated union narrowing relies on the discriminant property having a literal type that matches one of the union members. When a value is constructed via a type assertion (`as`), excess property checking is suppressed and missing required properties are not flagged. After narrowing, the compiler treats the object as fully satisfying all properties of the narrowed union member, even if those properties are missing at runtime.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Circle = { kind: \"circle\"; radius: number };\ntype Square = { kind: \"square\"; side: number };\ntype Shape = Circle | Square;\n\nfunction area(shape: Shape): number {\n    if (shape.kind === \"circle\") {\n        return Math.PI * shape.radius ** 2; // radius may be undefined at runtime\n    }\n    return shape.side ** 2;\n}\n\n// Type assertion bypasses structural checking for `radius`\nconst broken = { kind: \"circle\" } as Shape;\narea(broken); // Narrowed to Circle, but radius is undefined → NaN at runtime\n```\n\n**Exhaustiveness checking defeated:**\n\n```typescript\ntype Result = { status: \"ok\"; data: string } | { status: \"error\"; message: string };\n\nfunction handle(r: Result): string {\n    switch (r.status) {\n        case \"ok\": return r.data.toUpperCase();\n        case \"error\": return r.message;\n        default:\n            const _never: never = r;\n            return \"\";\n    }\n}\n\n// Assertion with widened discriminant bypasses exhaustiveness\nconst bad = { status: \"ok\" as string, data: 42 } as Result;\nhandle(bad); // Enters \"ok\" branch, calls toUpperCase() on number 42 → RuntimeError\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`narrowTypeByDiscriminant` trusts the static type of the discriminant property. Type assertions (`as`) in `src/compiler/checker.ts` (`checkAsExpression`) perform only a subset of structural checks and suppress excess property errors. The narrowed type then claims full structural satisfaction of the union member.\n\n**Severity:** **LOW–MEDIUM**\nRequires prior use of `as`. Common in practice for test fixtures, mock objects, and working around overly strict types.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Warn when a type assertion is applied to a discriminated union type and the asserted concrete member has properties that are structurally absent from the source type.\n- Emit a note at discriminant narrowing sites when the value's declared type was produced via an assertion.\n\n---\n\n### Finding 5: Double Type Assertion Cascading into Narrowing (High Severity)\n\n**Functions involved:** `checkAsExpression`, `narrowType`\n\n**Description:**\nTypeScript intentionally permits the \"double cast\" pattern (`x as unknown as T`) as an escape hatch for cases where the programmer knows the type is correct but the compiler cannot verify it. Once this assertion is applied, all subsequent narrowing operates on the asserted (potentially false) type as ground truth. There is no record of the assertion chain, so the unsoundness is invisible to downstream code.\n\n**Code Example:**\n\n```typescript\n// Dangerous: unique symbol brand is supposed to be unforgeable\ninterface SecureToken {\n    readonly __tokenBrand: unique symbol;\n    readonly value: string;\n}\n\ndeclare function useToken(token: SecureToken): void;\n\n// Bypass the unique symbol brand with a double assertion\nconst fakeToken = \"malicious-string\" as unknown as SecureToken;\nuseToken(fakeToken); // Fully type-safe per TypeScript, brand was never created\n```\n\n**Cascading narrowing after double assertion:**\n\n```typescript\nfunction processShape(x: string | number): void {\n    if (typeof x === \"string\") {\n        // x is narrowed to string\n        const corrupted = x as unknown as { exec: () => string };\n        // TypeScript now reasons about corrupted as { exec: () => string }\n        // All subsequent narrowing applied to `corrupted` is invalid\n        corrupted.exec(); // Runtime TypeError\n    }\n}\n```\n\n**Root Cause:**\n`checkAsExpression` (`src/compiler/checker.ts`) permits any expression to be asserted as any type as long as one side is assignable to or from the other, OR as long as both sides go through `unknown` (the double-cast trick). Once asserted, the narrowing functions (`narrowType`, etc.) have no way to detect that the type's provenance was an assertion rather than a sound derivation.\n\n**Severity:** **HIGH**\nThe double-assertion pattern is common in TypeScript codebases and is treated as an escape hatch. However, it fundamentally breaks the type system's soundness guarantees. Security-sensitive patterns that rely on `unique symbol` brands (capability-based security, CSRF tokens, sanitized strings) are particularly vulnerable.\n\n**Recommendations:**\n- Add a `--warnOnDoubleAssertion` flag to emit a warning for `x as unknown as T` patterns.\n- Explore \"sealing\" unique-symbol branded types so they cannot be produced via assertion.\n- Consider tracking assertion provenance in the type system so that narrowing can optionally refuse to further refine assertion-derived types.\n\n---\n\n## Summary Table\n\n| # | Pattern | Primary Functions | Severity |\n|---|---------|-------------------|----------|\n| 1 | User-defined type guard lying | `narrowTypeByGuard`, `getTypePredicateOfSignature` | **HIGH** |\n| 2 | Control flow narrowing lost across function boundaries | `getFlowTypeOfReference`, FlowCall handling | **MEDIUM** |\n| 3 | `typeof` narrowing with branded types | `narrowTypeByTypeof` | **MEDIUM** |\n| 4 | Discriminated union + type assertion | `narrowTypeByDiscriminant`, `checkAsExpression` | **LOW–MEDIUM** |\n| 5 | Double type assertion cascading into narrowing | `checkAsExpression`, `narrowType` | **HIGH** |\n\n---\n\n## Architectural Observations\n\n1. **TypeFacts are internally sound.** The `TypeFacts` bitmask system (used throughout narrowing, e.g., `TypeFacts.EQUndefined` at line ~11042 in `checker.ts`) correctly encodes runtime type semantics. Unsoundness arises not from TypeFacts themselves but from the trust placed in user-provided evidence (type guards, assertions).\n\n2. **`IncompleteType` is safely contained.** Loop analysis uses `IncompleteType` objects (`src/compiler/types.ts`, lines 4252–4259) internally within `getFlowTypeOfReference`. These have `flags = 0` and are explicitly documented as never escaping the function — no security concern.\n\n3. **Interprocedural analysis is deliberately limited.** TypeScript does not analyze function bodies at call sites. `FlowCall` nodes (`src/compiler/binder.ts`, line ~534) trigger conservative widening, but the analysis does not track aliased writes through closures or objects. This is a deliberate scalability trade-off that creates narrowing soundness gaps.\n\n4. **`strictNullChecks` is a major defense boundary.** Programs not using `--strictNullChecks` have significantly weaker guarantees. All null/undefined-related narrowing unsoundness is amplified without this flag.\n\n5. **Type assertions are the root of most unsoundness.** The `as` operator in `checkAsExpression` is the primary source of narrowing unsoundness because it allows injecting arbitrary types into the system. All five findings above either directly involve assertions or are exacerbated by them.\n","structuredPatch":[],"originalFile":null}}
